quick_facts.label: Primary linked qualification
quick_facts.value: The National Council for Hotel Management and Catering Technology (NCHMCT) lists the Diploma in Food Production as a 1.5-year programme; its published eligibility criteria specify a pass in Senior Secondary (10+2) or an equivalent examination.

quick_facts.label: Training emphasis
quick_facts.value: NCHMCT describes the Diploma in Food Production as practical, kitchen- and laboratory-based training, with 36 weeks of instruction followed by 24 weeks of industrial training.

quick_facts.label: Food-safety context
quick_facts.value: In India, licensed catering food businesses must comply with Schedule 4 hygiene requirements; Part 5 specifically covers catering operations.

quick_facts.label: Typical employing settings
quick_facts.value: NCHMCT identifies hotels, restaurants, fast-food outlets, resorts, railways, airlines, cruise liners and allied hospitality industries as establishments requiring trained food-production personnel.

sections.heading: What a Catering Assistant does
paragraphs: A Catering Assistant is an operational food-production support worker. In an institutional kitchen, catering unit or event-food operation, the role typically involves assisting the kitchen team with preparation and service workflow, keeping work areas, equipment and utensils clean, and following instructions for safe use of kitchen tools and equipment.

Food safety and sanitation are central rather than incidental responsibilities. FSSAI requires licensed catering businesses to follow the catering-specific hygiene and sanitary requirements in Part 5 of Schedule 4, within a documented Food Safety Management System. Assistants therefore need to follow the employer’s procedures for personal hygiene, cleaning, safe handling, waste disposal and food-service controls.

The Directorate General of Training’s Food Production (General) curriculum reinforces these operational expectations through training in kitchen-process flow, hygiene and cleanliness, safe handling of knives and equipment, protective dress, first aid, emergency preparedness and waste-management procedures.
bullets: Support food preparation, mise en place and kitchen-service workflow under the direction of cooks or supervisors.
Clean and maintain kitchen work areas, cooking utensils and service equipment according to workplace procedures.
Use knives, hand tools and kitchen equipment safely; wear required protective clothing.
Follow hygiene, sanitation, waste-disposal and emergency procedures that support the caterer’s food-safety controls.

sections.heading: Entry route through Diploma in Food Production
paragraphs: For this node, the directly linked education route is the Diploma in Food Production. NCHMCT’s published 2026 eligibility criteria state that the diploma is 1.5 years and requires Senior Secondary (10+2) or an equivalent qualification. Candidates should still verify the current admission notice, selection process, fees and any institute-specific conditions with the individual affiliated Institute of Hotel Management.

NCHMCT describes the programme as combining food-production instruction with hands-on kitchen and laboratory learning, followed by industrial training. Its syllabus covers cookery, larder work, hygiene and sanitation, nutrition, commodities and food costing, alongside practical cookery and larder training.

For learners seeking a foundation before or instead of the diploma, NCHMCT also lists a 1.5-year Craftsmanship Certificate Course in Food Production for candidates who have passed Class 10 or equivalent. This is a separate credential, not a substitute for checking the employer’s stated qualification requirements.
bullets: Complete 10+2 or equivalent for the NCHMCT Diploma in Food Production route.
Build practical capability through institute kitchens, laboratories and industrial training.
Check the specific institute’s current notice for admissions, fees, seat availability and selection rules.
Consider a Class-10-entry craftsmanship certificate in food production as an alternative foundational training route.

sections.heading: Skills to develop
paragraphs: The role calls for practical kitchen discipline: hygiene and cleaning, safe use of knives and equipment, correct use of protective clothing, basic first aid awareness, waste management and readiness for emergencies. These are explicit learning areas in the Directorate General of Training’s Food Production (General) trade syllabus.

Food-safety literacy is especially valuable in catering. FSSAI defines food handlers as people who directly handle packaged or unpackaged food, food-preparation equipment, utensils or food-contact surfaces. Its FoSTaC materials emphasize hygienic and sanitary practices, personal hygiene and safe food handling; training may be relevant where an employer assigns staff to food-safety responsibilities.
bullets: Kitchen hygiene, cleaning and sanitation discipline.
Safe knife, hand-tool and kitchen-equipment handling.
Personal protective clothing, grooming and safe working habits.
Waste handling, emergency awareness and basic first-aid response.
Clear coordination with cooks, supervisors and service teams during time-sensitive production and service.

sections.heading: Where the role can be found
paragraphs: Catering Assistant work is generally performed in food-production environments rather than solely at a customer-facing counter. NCHMCT identifies hospitality employers needing trained food-production staff across hotels, restaurants, fast-food outlets, resorts, railways, airlines and cruise liners, alongside allied industries. In practice, the same support skills can be used in caterers’ production kitchens and institutional food-service operations where the employer’s food-safety procedures apply.

The work environment can involve prolonged standing, heat, sharp tools, cleaning activity and coordinated peak-period production. The DGT curriculum’s emphasis on personal safety, protective clothing, fire awareness, emergency exits and use of extinguishers indicates the operational safety demands of kitchen work.
bullets: Rail, airline and cruise-related catering operations.
Caterers and other allied hospitality food-production settings.
